result:
In the entryway, a chipped ceramic bowl keeps a congress of keys. They lean against one another like neighbors at a fence, cool small teeth glinting when morning light pushes across the rug. None of them are labeled, and yet the hand knows them. It knows the soft bend of the brass that opens the garden gate, the stubborn ridges of the deadbolt, the absurdly thin skeleton that might not open anything at all but refuses to be thrown away. Every house is a pact; every key is a promise that you may step out and return. The bowl was your grandmother’s, ringed with blue poppies that have washed to a pale tide over decades of sinks and moves and winters that made everything in the house sound like a whisper. The bowl is too heavy for its job. That is part of the point.

The kettle breathes on the stove, offering its first thin rumor of steam. You think of all the small metals in a morning: the curved spine of a spoon, the lip of the faucet, the hinge that doesn’t squeak because someone once rubbed the candle’s stub along it and called that good work. If there is a theology you subscribe to, it has to do with these nearly invisible things that hold and give and break only in slow confession. You drank your coffee black for a year when the doctor frowned, and then drifted back to cream, to the little cloud of sweetness that announces its arrival with the scent of vanilla and a note that is nearly caramel. A key turns in a lock and you don’t think of the lock; you think of the opening. We have told ourselves the story of doors so well that we rarely look at the hinges.

There’s a shop in the square where you can have a key copied, a boot resoled, a watch’s pulse restored. The man who works there has a generous silence, like a deep pocket you could trust with a note, a ring, a last-ditch hope. He moves without hurry, his hands marked with a grayish dust that no soap can persuade to leave. He holds old watches to his ear as if listening for a whispered confession, then exhales and lays them open like small, polished towns. The floor smells like leather and warmed metal. Children press their faces to the glass and watch the duplicator chew along a blank key’s edge, sparks occasionally stitching light along the bench. He could be a priest if the world had found him sooner or if he had been born into a mythology sturdier than retail. As it is, he collects thresholds.

Once, a woman came with a paper bag full of keys and said, I can’t throw them away. Each one gleamed like a tiny argument. What doors? he asked, not looking up as if the question were too tender to be met eye to eye. She shrugged, smiled like someone trying to be brave for a child, and said, Dorm rooms, storage units, a mailbox that may now be a mailbox only in memory. He took one, ran his thumb along its spine, held it to the light and read the cuts as if they were letters you could rearrange into a life. In another age, he might have been a cartographer who drew maps of coasts he’d never seen by copying the curl of a gull’s wing. He swept the bag’s thin mouth closed and suggested a ritual: pick one key, choose a door that has become a ghost, and bury both beneath a forgiving tree.

Overhead in late September, the migrating birds write an equation you can’t solve but whose answer you have always lived within. They lift from the salt marshes just after the sun gives up its copper and go dark together, a silence you feel in your teeth. People say they steer by the magnet of the earth, by stars, by a sense of falling toward home that cannot be taught but can be inherited. Roads are a human boast; currents are the world’s older grammar. You crouch at the shore and watch the tiny crabs tap their chalky stories in the sand, the wrack line bowed with eelgrass and feathers. If you once learned how to tell time by the migration, you have forgotten. It isn’t a forgetting that stings. It is the generous sort that trusts the sky to remember you.

You learned to ride a bicycle late. The street gently sloped toward a neighbor’s forsythia, an amber fountain assembled from a thousand delicate suns. The first time you stopped falling, your knees felt like loose bolts. The air smelled like tar and lilac. Someone cheered too loudly from a porch and you hated how good it felt. You tasted iron where you bit your lip and thought, so this is what blood is for: a red autograph on the world to certify that you were here, moving, briefly balanced on a question. Brakes squealed; the chain clicked; you became one more mechanism, a child-sized engine turning the spring into a choir of blackbirds fleeing the hedge. The body is a machine that likes to believe in love. The machine works best when it does.

There was a storm once that pressed the town flat. The kind of storm that rearranges names. You recognized the end of it not by the quiet—storms teach you a new kind of quiet—but by the sound of people locking and unlocking things again, testing what still answered. The square lost power, and the locksmith’s shop went soft and dark. People drifted in on the damp smell of the street, and he lit a small candle warped from years beside the register. Watch hands lay slack at their posts; a velvet pad threw back its shine. Someone laughed in the foolish, relieved way we laugh when it turns out the end of the world is only a blackout. He told a story then, not to pass the time but to touch it. About a lighthouse whose keeper wrote letters to the ships he couldn’t see, every night, in longhand. Dear Vessel of Unknown Name, he would begin, if you can read this, then ink can fly. He tucked their unwritten replies in a tin. Storms came. Men came. Wars arrived. The tin went with him into the ground. What remained was the habit of attention, which is also a key.

When power returned, it did so in a shy shrug. Fluorescents stuttered awake; the bulletin board rediscovered its color. People found reasons to stay a little longer anyway. A boy spun a tin tag on the counter and called it a comet, a coin, a planet with a history hard to fold into a pocket. The locksmith wound the old clocks, one by one. Their ticks did not agree, but they harmonized, like a field of crickets whose quarrel is their chorus. Outside, puddles grew eyes and streets remembered how to glitter. You could feel the town relatch around you, a ship’s hatch spun snug against weather. Even the trees had that particular exhausted posture that means we lived, we kept standing.

At home, the bowl was a nebula of metal and lint. You turned the key of your own door because belonging is a practice more than a right. Later, over dishes, you rehearsed the inventory of your day the way you inventory small losses because each tells you what you meant to keep: a name almost remembered, a headline that dulled the knife and so was not read; a single dandelion mowing down the lawn’s idea of itself. Somewhere a train processioned across the night, its horn unwinding the town thread by careful thread so it could lace it again. You thought of doors that never learned the word no. You thought of the birds whose map is a pull in the blood, their keys internal, weightless, shining only to themselves.

If you go out, lock the door softly. If you return late, let your shoes invent a way to be quiet. Leave a spare beneath the honest stone that looks like a turtle. Remember the neighbor who knocks not because the package is yours but because the knock is. When the kettle breathes, answer in kind. If you misplace what lets you in, wait on the stoop with your empty hands and your name. Someone will pass with a story that fits. Someone will say, try this. Someone will show you the small hinge in the universe that opens without any metal at all, and it will feel like hunger, and it will feel like grace.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
